cards.text: The end of the magnet that points towards north direction is called the North-seeking pole or the North pole of the magnet.
The other end that points towards the South direction is called the South-seeking pole or the South pole of the magnet.

Reshma the Storyteller
Reshma lives in a coastal town of Kerala and enjoys writing short stories. These stories are loved by her grandmother, especially on special occasions.

Special Story
Inspired by the trade history of Kerala, Reshma writes about a ship carrying spices and explores challenges faced by sailors.

Celestial Navigation
In Reshma's story, sailors use stars at night to navigate. These stars served as guides for ancient mariners.

The Sudden Storm
Reshma crafts a suspenseful moment where sailors are caught in a storm with an overcast sky, making stars invisible.

Research and Discovery
Needing solutions, Reshma delves into the internet and library to discover navigational tools used in history.

The Magnetic Compass
Reshma learns about the magnetic compass—a crucial invention that helped sailors find directions without stars.

Everyday Magnets
Reshma realizes she encounters magnets daily—from pencil boxes and purses to magnetic board dusters.

Lodestones and History
Natural magnets, known as lodestones, fascinated ancient people and were a key discovery in making navigational magnets.

Iron and Artificial Magnets
Beyond natural magnets, Reshma learns people discovered making magnets from iron, leading to modern artificial magnets.

Shapes and Diversity
Magnets in classrooms vary in shape and use—found in everyday objects like toys and tools (as shown in Fig. 4.1 & 4.2).

Magnetic Materials
Materials like iron, nickel, and cobalt are magnetic and are often used in making magnets.

Understanding Magnetism
Reshma learns that not all materials are magnetic—those not attracted to magnets are termed non-magnetic.

Activity 4.1 Exploration
In her exploration, Reshma engages in hands-on activities to identify magnetic and non-magnetic materials.

Magnetic Combinations
Certain combinations of metals with iron, nickel, or cobalt also exhibit magnetic properties.

Application in Education
From school labs to stories, Reshma combines creativity with learning, crafting tales rooted in scientific discovery.

Iron Filings and Magnetism
The majority of iron filings adhere near the ends of a bar magnet. These concentrations are at the poles.

Definition of Poles
The ends of a magnet are known as the two poles: the North pole and the South pole.

Iron Filings at Poles
Most iron filings tend to stick to the poles regardless of the shape of the magnet.

Impossibility of a Single Pole Magnet
It is not possible to create a magnet exhibiting only a single pole.

Breaking Magnets
  • When a magnet is broken into pieces, each piece retains both a North and a South pole.
  • Even the smallest fragment will still have both poles.

Concept of Magnetic Poles
Magnetic poles always exist in pairs, no matter the size of the magnet.

Sticking Properties of Magnets
Iron filings primarily attach to the poles, highlighting the strength and concentration of the magnetic field there.

Magnet Shape Irrelevance
The shape of a magnet does not affect the characteristic of poles attracting more iron filings.

Magnetic Field Visualization
Using iron filings helps visualize the concentrated strength of the magnetic field at the poles.

General Magnet Properties
  • All magnets exhibit this basic property of dual poles.
  • Break a magnet, and you still find both poles in the resulting pieces.

Magnet Direction
A freely suspended magnet aligns itself along the north-south direction. The north-pointing end is known as the North pole, and the other end is the South pole.

Earth's Magnetism
The Earth's magnetic field causes magnets to align in the north-south direction, illustrating that Earth behaves like a giant magnet.

Iron Bar Experiment
Replacing a bar magnet with a small iron bar shows that non-magnetic objects do not consistently rest along the north-south direction.

Testing for Magnetism
The alignment property of magnets can be used to determine if a piece of metal is magnetized.

Magnetic Compass Invention
The compass utilizes a magnet to find directions, using a needle that spins freely to align in the north-south direction.

Compass Usage
Placing the compass at a location allows the needle to rest in a north-south orientation, with directions indicated on the dial.

Compass Structure
A compass typically contains:
  • A small circular box with a clear cover
  • A needle-shaped magnet mounted on a pin
  • A dial with marked directions

Needle Movement
The compass needle is balanced on a pin to rotate freely and accurately indicate direction.

Needle Coloring
For clarity, the end of the compass needle pointing north is usually painted red.

The matsya-yantra
Ancient Indians used a navigation device with a fish-shaped magnetized iron piece floating in oil, called matsya-yantra.

Old vs Modern Compasses
Before modern compasses, devices like the matsya-yantra served a similar purpose for seafaring navigation.

Magnet Needle Mechanics
The needle's free movement is crucial for determining direction accurately, aligning with the Earth's magnetic field.

Compass Dial Usage
Rotate the compass box so that the dial's north and south align with the needle to find all directions at a location.

Importance of Magnetization
Recognizing that only magnets align in specific directions helps distinguish magnets from ordinary metal objects.

Construction of a Magnetic Needle
Crafting a magnetic needle from simple materials demonstrates basic compass mechanics similar to historical devices.
Magnetic Attraction and Repulsion - The compass needle functions as a magnet. - Magnets have both North and South poles, which influence each other's movement.
Concept of Magnetic Poles - Like poles repel each other. - Opposite poles attract each other.
Behavior of Compass Needle with Magnets - When the North pole of a magnet approaches, the compass needle's North pole moves away.
Understanding Magnetic Interaction - The North pole of a magnet repels the North pole of another magnet like a compass needle.
Closer Proximity Induces Closer Movement - The South pole of a magnet, when brought near the North pole of a compass needle, causes the needle to move closer.
Magnetic Poles Reaction Summary - Combining magnets: 1. North-North: Repulsion occurs. 2. South-North: Attraction occurs.
Clear Examples of Magnetic Influence - Experimenting with magnet poles can illustrate the basic principles of magnetism using the compass and magnets.
Magnetism in Everyday Tools - A compass uses basic magnetism principles to point direction, aligning itself with Earth's magnetic field.
Magnetic Movement Magnets can move some objects without touching them.

Identification of Magnet Poles Magnets often have their poles marked.
  • The North pole may be marked as 'N'.
  • The South pole may be marked as 'S'.
  • Often, a white dot indicates the North pole.
  • Sometimes, red paint is used for the North pole and blue for the South pole.

Proper Magnet Storage Magnets should be stored correctly to maintain their magnetism.
  • Store magnets in pairs with unlike poles on the same side.
  • Place a piece of wood between them.
  • Position two pieces of soft iron across the ends to stabilize the field.

Handling Caution Avoid certain actions to keep magnets functional.
  • Do not heat, drop, or hammer the magnet.
  • Avoid placing magnets near mobile phones or remote controls.
Poles of a Magnet A magnet always has two poles: North and South. These exist in pairs.
Impossibility of Isolated Magnetic Poles It is impossible to have an isolated North or South pole on its own.

Magnetic and Non-Magnetic Materials Materials are categorized based on their reaction to magnets.
  • Magnetic materials are attracted to magnets.
  • Non-magnetic materials are not attracted to magnets.
Behavior of Suspended Magnets When suspended freely, a magnet will always align with the north-south direction.
Magnetic Compass Function A magnetic compass needle aligns along the north-south direction due to Earth's magnetic field.
Interaction of Like Poles Like poles of magnets (North-North, South-South) repel when brought near each other.
Interaction of Unlike Poles Unlike poles of magnets (North-South) attract each other when close.

Visual Identification of Magnet Poles
  • Some magnets are color-coded to quickly identify the poles during use.
Earth's Magnetic Influence Suspension aligns the magnet with Earth's magnetic field, aiding in direction finding.
Role of Soft Iron in Magnet Storage Soft iron helps in maintaining magnetism by closing the magnetic circuit.
Magnet-Friendly Storage Techniques Strategically placing wood and soft iron can sustain the magnet's polarity and effectiveness.
A magnet has two poles - the North pole and the South pole.
The poles of a magnet always exist in pairs. A single North pole or a single South pole cannot exist.
Magnetic materials are the materials that are attracted towards a magnet.
A freely suspended magnet rests along the north-south direction.
The needle of a magnetic compass indicates the north-south direction.
When two magnets are brought close to each other, like poles (North-North, South-South) repel each other while unlike poles (North-South) attract each other.
